text: Through the analysis of the disaster-forming conditions, disaster risk levels, and disaster-bearing conditions of the Haikou coastal zone, the fuzzy mathematics method was adopted to study coastal intrusion and conduct a comprehensive assessment of the risk of erosion disasters, determining the risk level and distribution range of coastal erosion disasters. The evaluation results were relatively satisfactory. The results of the risk and vulnerability assessment show that the high-risk areas of coastal erosion in Haikou are distributed along some sections of Haikou Holiday Beach and Changbin North Fifth Road. Partial sections of the banks along the Wuyuan River Estuary and the Nandu River eastward to Dayuan Village are also key areas for the prevention and control of coastal erosion disasters in the future. To prevent further deterioration of high-risk areas and maintain the normal production and life of coastal residents, the following measures are proposed: (1) Strictly prohibit sand mining near and along the shore. Competent authorities should strengthen supervision over reclamation projects to prevent new illegal and non-compliant sand mining at sea. (2) Strictly prohibit sand mining in rivers. Relevant government departments should take joint actions and accelerate the introduction of corresponding river sand management policies to prevent uncontrolled sand mining in the Nandu River Basin, ensuring the normal flow of sediment from rivers into the sea and preventing coastal erosion and retreat. (3) Actively carry out rectification and restoration work in high-risk sections of coastal erosion. Severe coastal erosion indicates that the coast in this area is already in a serious state of danger and cannot restore itself naturally. Relevant authorities should identify the main causes of coastal erosion and carry out targeted rectification and restoration work.

Chinese loess is renowned worldwide for its complete strata, large thickness, wide distribution and special engineering properties. On the plane, it extends outward from the 'Central Loess Plateau' mainly composed of Shanxi, Shaanxi and Gansu, covering an area of 6.3×105 square kilometers. The arid and semi-arid climate environment of the local area during the Quaternary period created conditions for the formation of material sources and the material transportation mainly caused by wind, as well as the accumulation and erosion of loess, resulting in the unique loess landform of today. In this process, landslides are both a result of geomorphic evolution and an active participant in the process. Therefore, it is believed that the unique particle composition and engineering properties of loess play an important role in the occurrence and evolution of loess landslides. Summarize the research achievements in this aspect and emphasize that these properties follow a directional gradual (improvement) rule from NW to SE. Based on this, the geomorphic zoning of the 'Loess Plateau', the distribution of loess landslides, the types of landslides and the formation mechanism of landslides are discussed. In the discussion of the inducing factors of landslides, the loess landslides caused by earthquakes are described with emphasis. In terms of landslide prevention and control, some engineering examples are used as a backdrop to summarize the commonly used and effective methods for treating loess landslides at present. Finally, a brief discussion will be made on several issues that need attention for research.

Capillary hypothesis: This hypothesis originated from Taisaki's capillary theory. Taisaki believes that the reason why moist sandy soil can stick together is that water accumulates at the contact points of particles due to discontinuous moisture in sandy soil. On the gas interface, it is produced by the surface tension of the capillary tube, and the normal force connects the sand and soil particles together. The United States has not only repeatedly affirmed the existence of capillary action but also pointed out that fine powder particles aggregate between particles. Both the figure and the flocculation diagram of the clay sheet are intergranular cements formed by the retreat of the capillary curve liquid level. Not only does it have capillary tension after being soaked in water, but the fine powder particles and clay flakes that have been lost and cemented will also dissipate the 'illusion.' According to scanning electron microscope observations, most of the clay particles in loess have coagulated into aggregates, which is why fine particles in the yellow soil appear to have an advantage. Loess is a typical unsaturated soil with very low water content, and there must be intergranular curved liquid levels. The aggregation of intergranular clay observed in electron microscope images also proves this point. However, the capillary hypothesis cannot explain why some loess becomes wetted while some does not or becomes sunken on the yellow surface, even though their water content and particle composition are basically the same. The difference lies in their pore ratios.

Salt dissolution hypothesis: Some salts in loess are readily soluble in water, while others are insoluble. When loess contains water at a lower level, soluble salts are in a microcrystalline state attached to the surface of the particles, playing a cementing role. When the salt crystals dissolve after being immersed in water, cementation is lost, causing the loess to sink. This forms the basis of the salt dissolution hypothesis. However, it is also believed that when the water content in loess is only about half, the soluble salts in loess are in a dissolved state at the capillary bend liquid surface, so solubility issues of readily soluble salts do not fully explain the phenomenon. According to the structural characteristics of natural loess observed by electron microscopy and specific surface measurement calculations, this inference is correct. However, the role of soluble salts cannot be denied. Even in a dissolved state, they affect the intergranular space and the magnitude of the forces acting. Immersion in water inevitably changes the concentration of electrolytes in the aqueous solution in the pores. At high electrolyte concentrations, the intergranular arch force increases, while at low concentrations, the intergranular repulsion force increases. Therefore, soaking in water causes a reduction in intergranular strength. There is thus a certain connection between water immersion, collapsibility, and the presence of dissolved salts in loess. But as mentioned earlier, this hypothesis cannot explain all subsidence phenomena.

There is not enough glue, and it is recognized that the collapsibility of loess is due to containing fewer particles smaller than a certain size. This type of soil lacks the colloidal dispersion part. When there is a sufficient quantity of colloid, the expansion process can prevent moisture from causing subsidence. However, the collapsible loess of Lanzhou, by its own weight, is dispersed by a dispersant. The content of sticky particles was found to be high, and beam diffraction analysis shows that the main mineral component adhering is water mica, with montmorillonite and kaolinite also present. This indicates that the collapsibility of loess does not depend solely on viscosity, composition, or content of soil minerals. The surface activity of clay in loess is controlled by calcium ions and microcrystalline calcium carbonate, which condense into aggregates.

The water film wedging diagram shows the different net potential energy of varying electrolyte concentrations. The intergranular adsorption water film can become thinner due to either a decrease in water content or an increase in intergranular pressure. The thinner the water film between particles, the more the particles coagulate and the higher the polymer strength. The thinning of the water film is due to the effect of hydration energy on the particle surface, which strives to restore the water film to its original thickness. Therefore, when the water content in the soil increases, water acts to wedge particles apart, increasing the distance between grains. As a result, the water film thickens, and the connection strength decreases. This view is supported by dipole forces. The surface activity of clay particles in the soil is not affected by calcium or other high-valent ions and can fully play its role. Yellow sticky particles are coagulated into aggregates by calcium ions. When the surface activity of clay particles is controlled by calcium ions, it is still difficult to determine how much effect the water film wedge can have. Even if it plays a certain role, it can only indicate that intergranular strength has decreased and cannot be considered the main cause of subsidence.

Structural theory: People have long noticed that the collapsible deformation of loess is related to its special structure. Lin Chongyi used magnifying observations and analyzed the role of various particle groups in the structure of loess, leading to the presentation of a schematic diagram of the loess structure model. After studying the microstructure of Soviet loess using a polarizing microscope, the structure of loess was divided into granular structures, collective structures, and granular-collective structures. Loess with a granular structure has high water resistance and cohesive force ratio, while yellow soil is low in cohesion but should have high water permeability. The open metastable structure of soil is considered the basic condition of collapsibility. Due to limitations of testing technology, many conclusions were largely inferential. With the development of modern science and technology, especially the application of scanning electron microscopy and the detection of micro-area components by beam spectroscopy, this theory achieved rapid development. By the end of the 1980s, research on the microstructure and collapsibility of yellow soil in our country had entered a new stage. Not only did it provide a convincing explanation of the mechanism of collapsibility of loess, but it also allowed loess to be classified by engineering geology according to its microstructure characteristics. This theory has taken a meaningful step from theoretical research to engineering application.

Theoretical calculations and experimental results show that it is feasible to study the collapsible deformation of loess using microstructure mutation loss and stability theory. This theory can explain many characteristics of collapsible deformation. Theoretical calculations indicate that collapsible deformation is highly sensitive to parameters. After immersion in water, even a slight weakening of K(w) can cause a significant increase in wetting, which essentially reflects microstructural sensitivity to defects. Both volumetric strain and octahedral shear strain are influenced by spherical stress and octahedral stress, with cross-effects of shear stress. The intersection of shear curves corresponding to different stress ratios, known as the fork phenomenon, is one of the physical essences of collapsible loess. The initial condition of depression can be expressed as an elliptic equation close to a circle in the p-q coordinate system.

Based on observation and analysis, and through discussions of various hypotheses, the author believes that the reasons for the subsidence of loess in Lanzhou are as follows. The loess in Lanzhou has a granular suspended structure, composed of particles with large pores. This structure has fewer intergranular contact points per unit volume, and the skeleton particles have a high degree of mobility, making them extremely easy to destabilize. Therefore, this is the fundamental condition for the formation of collapsibility. The skeletal framework particles of loess, apart from detrital minerals, include an inner layer formed by the aggregation of sticky powder particles, creating aggregates with relatively high cementation strength. Under compressive force or immersion in water, structural deformation is mainly due to the fracture of intergranular contact cementation. Therefore, the intergranular contact cementation strength is key to ensuring the stability of this structure.The cementation properties between grains of the loess framework are complex. According to their reactions to water, they can be classified into water-stabilized cementation and non-water-stabilized cementation. Under drought conditions, non-water-stable adhesions are generally dominant, while under moist conditions, water-stabilized bonding predominates. Capillary gelation and salt dissolution crystallization belong to non-water-stabilized gelation, whereas iron oxides and aged carbonate bonds belong to water-stabilized bonds. The adhesion between water-stable and non-water-stable cement is determined by the water film wedge, the concentration and composition of ions in the pore solution, and colloid chemical changes. The loess in Lanzhou is mainly cemented by non-water-stabilized cement, and the wedging effect of the water film facilitates the dissolution of the adhesive film, causing intergranular gelation strength to drop sharply after water immersion.The water-stabilized bonding strength between particles, sometimes called residual strength, represents the remaining strength under water immersion conditions. The external compressive force that overcomes this residual strength is called the initial wetting compressive force. In Lanzhou loess, the proportion of water-stabilized cementation strength is very small, and even under the soil’s own weight, it can be easily overcome. Therefore, this type of yellow soil exhibits its characteristic heavy moisture subsidence.Although the content and composition of clay minerals in general cohesive soil significantly affect its engineering properties, in Lanzhou loess, the main factor determining collapsibility is the occurrence state of clay particles. Finally, the author believes that the special structure of Lanzhou loess, the occurrence state of clay particles, and the local natural and geographical conditions are closely related to the formation of wind-deposited soil. However, the exact formation process is worth further discussion.

Wet sink deformation consists of two parts: wet sink volume deformation and shear deformation. When K is large, it mainly manifests as wet sink volume deformation; when K is small, it mainly manifests as wet sink shear deformation. The structure of loess is damaged and its strength decreases during water immersion, similar to softening. After wetting, the volume is compacted, and the formation of new structural strength is similar to hardening. 2. Collapsible deformation is closely related to the stress state and stress path, and is cross-influenced by the mean stress P and shear stress q. The quantitative relationship between them can be characterized by hyperbolas and power functions. 3. When calculating the collapsible deformation of the foundation, the foundation should be zoned according to the stress state and deformation conditions in the foundation, and different test indicators should be adopted for different areas. It is recommended to use triaxial test indicators within the range of the compression layer and lateral limit compression test indicators below the compression layer.

The structural analysis of the loading and seepage process of loess using CT technology is a highly effective and innovative method. A number of experiments were carried out on the original loess. Meaningful dynamic scanning experiments can be obtained from CT images and data analysis to observe the change process of the unearthed samples intuitively. It can be analyzed that this is due to the presence of loess loose capillary pores, which are formed due to the capillary affinity for water and the infiltration pathways of water. The infiltration of water eventually broke down the soil framework, filled all the larger holes, and caused the loess to sink. The action of the load causes the loess in the direction of loading to compress, rapidly expand the fissure, and cause the specimen to fail. On the side, under certain circumstances, the soil sample becomes dense, the soil particles redistribute, and lose their original state characteristics of loess, making it hard for water to penetrate. In engineering practice, considering the precipitation volume and precipitation density, protective measures can be based on the actual situation of the project. Samples of soil quality and engineering environmental conditions were taken for targeted CT scans to observe the stability of the soil structure and determine the safety factor.

Anayev of the Soviet Union once proposed using collapsibility potential energy to evaluate the collapsibility of loess. The fundamental concept of this method is that soil with high porosity has the possibility of being compacted in volume. That is to say, it has a component that can change the mechanical potential energy on the other side. Yellow soil has the potential to absorb water. The price can be evaluated based on an index that depends on the porosity and moisture content of the loess, reflecting the collapsibility of loess. A point from the stress-humidified deformation surface of the figure, when loading and humidifying, different stress humidification paths can be passed through to reach the final point of saturated humidification at the given stress level. The total deformation from the point to the point is called 'Yin Er Yi'. Stress increase wet paths can vary, but the total deformation is always a constant value. The point can be regarded as the extreme value of deformation stability. At this point, stress has reached the given value, the water content has already reached the corresponding maximum water supply content of field two, and both loading deformation and humidification deformation are stable. Therefore, a point can be regarded as the basis for deformation stability. The opposite deformation potential and the opposite temperature collapse potential at this point are both equal to zero. If the stress of the graph is humidified to deform the surface, it can be regarded as a vertical downward coordinate axis. The point is the position on the curved surface, the lowest end point. From any point on the force-induced humidification deformation surface, all can approach the point along different stress humidification paths. Thus, it can be considered that a certain point on a curved surface has a fixed and variable form pattern corresponding to the point. This change of situation can also be regarded as being caused by two factors: loading and heating, which are respectively called the loading deformation potential and the humidification deformation potential. The latter is also simply referred to as the collapsible potential. The position of the collapsible yellow on the stress-humidification deformation surface depends on the stress state and the water content once determined. There is a certain changing situation, the magnitude of which depends on the size of the given stress water level that can be achieved. This change is manifested as either loading deformation or humidification deformation, depending on the stress humidification path. If it is pre-humidification, the path of response is the middle mark of saturated humidification deformation. It is a deformation under load. If it is post-wet stress humidification path, it is a deformation under load and saturated humidification deformation. The path follows the stress humidification path and will manifest as the greatest collapsibility, and along the path, it will exhibit the smallest collapsibility potential. Such as the 'corpse point' and the 'tricky point' recombination, that is, the initial state corresponding to the natural water content. In the ever-changing situation, the path should be moistened with force after the knife is cut, and the collapsibility will reach its maximum value. However, all the prehumidification in the force heating path is manifested as loading deformation, and the collapsibility is equal to zero. The latter is equivalent to the deformation properties of saturated loess formed after collapsible loess is soaked and humidified. The overall variation form from the corpse point to the point and the nature of the soil, in particular, the density-pore ratio, is related to humidity. If a corpse point is used, it represents the total variation form of a corpse point under a given stress. It is the pore ratio corresponding to the state of the corpse point. The second is corresponding to the force, the maximum water immersion content under action, and the specific gravity of soil particles is the saturation of water immersion. The total deformation potential evidence is related to the given stress level and increases as it grows.

The influence of dehumidification and humidification on the evaluation of collapsibility of loess has already been elaborated in detail on the humidification deformation mechanism of collapsible loess in another article, Chuan Zhong. Collapsible loess, when first increasing in humidity, will cause the wet sink deformation to transform into loading deformation; that is, the collapsibility deteriorates while the compressibility increases. Through this experiment, another result in another direction was obtained: collapsible loess after pre-dehumidification, loading deformation will transform into collapsible deformation; that is, the compressibility decreases while the collapsibility intensifies. The transformation of these two deformations is largely reversible. Some sections of a certain construction site were originally a vegetable plot. Due to the influence of irrigation, the moisture content of the soil within a certain depth range of this section has increased, and the corresponding collapsibility coefficient is relatively low. The collapsibility of loess is evaluated based on the natural moisture content of the soil at the time of investigation. The graded subsidence in irrigated areas is lower than that in non-irrigated areas. But considering that the site has already been requisitioned, when irrigation is stopped, the moisture content of the soil will decrease due to evaporation and dehumidification; at this point, the collapsibility will improve.

Determination of collapsibility under high pressure: In existing engineering practice, it has been found that some yellow surfaces determined to be non-collapsible under normal pressure according to the specifications, the collapsibility coefficient obtained after immersion in water under high pressure may reach or exceed expected values. Through this experimental research, it is necessary to indicate that it cannot be assumed that the collapsibility coefficient always increases with the increase of pressure in all cases. The concept of termination pressure is shown in the figure, which can illustrate the initial density of soil under certain conditions, initial moisture content, structural strength, etc. Excessive pressure will compact the soil, the loading deformation will complete a major portion of the total deformation, and the collapsibility of the soil will be relatively weakened, potentially to the extent of complete disappearance. However, the total deformation of loess under high pressure will be relatively large, and this should be considered separately in engineering practice.

According to the microstructural characteristics of the suspended large pores in collapsible loess, it is proposed that the collapsible deformation is caused by the instability of the microstructure. The microstructure instability is simplified to one type: 'sharp point' disaster model, which leads to its damage evolution equation. The deformation of collapsible loess is decomposed into volume change and shear deformation, and the small deformation theory is still adopted. The volume deformation is the main part of the deformation described by microstructure instability, and the shear deformation relies on the constitutive theory of granular media. The two are combined to provide the complete constitutive equation of loess. The axisymmetric compression deformation law calculated according to the theory in this paper is basically consistent with the experimental results, and it can reflect 'scissor swelling' and 'cutting and editing' effects. The results of this paper show that recognizing the mechanism of loess collapsibility deformation from the perspective of microstructural stability damage is an effective approach.

(1) The thickness of the loess layer at the test site this time is greater than 60 meters, and the collapsible thickness of the loess layer is approximately 35 meters. The water immersion test pit is 110 meters long and 70 meters wide. Leaching water observation lasted for 162 days, and the observation after the water supply was cut off lasted for 62 days. Total water consumption was 124,043.2 m³, with the maximum daily water consumption being 3,700 cubic meters. The ground outside the pit is sinking, generally 30 to 40 meters outside the pit edge and in contact with the collapsible soil layer of the site. The thickness is close; the scale of the test is rare both at home and abroad, and valuable data and experience have been accumulated in the foundation treatment of heavily collapsible loess.(2) The self-weight collapsibility of the pump station foundation after treatment has been completely eliminated. The collapsibility under a pressure of 200 kPa has been basically eliminated, achieving the expected goal.(3) Due to the large thickness of the collapsible loess layer at the test site, due to the large area and strong collapsible nature of the test pit, it is recommended to level it for five consecutive days. Collapsible loess with a uniform collapsibility not exceeding 2 mm/d is classified as large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess. This is the standard for stopping water injection in the site immersion test. Although the suggested standard exceeded the relevant provisions of the 'Code for Construction in Collapsible Loess Areas,' through the approval of previous on-site water immersion test results, foundation pressure of buildings at this site, construction, a comprehensive analysis of the application conditions of the building and the time required for site dewatering, as well as the good effect after the base treatment, indicates that this suggested standard is reasonable and feasible. It can be used as a reference for similar foundation treatment projects and the revision of the loess code in the future. The adoption of this standard shortened the construction period and saved costs.(4) The amount of collapsibility of large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess varies with the duration of immersion. The development process consists of five stages: the initial gentle section, the submerged steep descent section, the mid-term smooth section, the steep descent section after water supply interruption, and the later smooth section. The width of the cracks around the test pit and the height difference of the ground on both sides of the cracks are much greater than previous similar research records. The corresponding variation characteristics of the subsidence rate are manifested as follows: during the immersion period, the rate of subsidence shows a change of 'small → large → small → stable.' After the water supply was cut off, the rate of subsidence showed a pattern of 'large → small → stable.' The rate of subsidence is relatively high, but it does not exceed that of the Donggang site in Lanzhou.(5) The planar influence distance outside the waterlogged pit of this site and the collapsible soil, the ratio of the depth to the lower limit of the layer, is approximately 90%, which is smaller than that in the Lanzhou area. This conforms to the general rules of Zone II.(6) The indoor test calculation of the self-weight collapsibility of this site is 103.1 cm. The on-site measured self-weight collapsible size was 115.7 cm, ranging from 163.0 to 261.1 cm. The site is located in Zone II of the collapsible loess engineering geological zoning, but the ground is considered. The correction coefficient βo for soil quality differences in the region is relatively high, generally ranging from 1.6 to 2.2, which is relatively close to the situation in Zone I.

Compression tests were conducted on unshaped loess from Xi 'an and Lanzhou under different moisture contents. A quantitative structural parameter mP was defined based on the pore ratio of unshaped loess to remolded saturated loess under the same pressure. The characteristics of the structural parameters of loess varying with pressure and moisture content w during moisture increase were explored. The mechanism of moisture increase deformation of unshaped loess was analyzed using the structural change characteristics. Research shows that :(1) Under the same pressure, the mP-w relationship can be divided into two segments: stable and decreasing, while the relationship between the humidification collapsion coefficient δs' and w can be divided into two segments: stable and increasing. The initial water content wf at the intersection of the humidification structure failure decreases with the increase of pressure. (2) At wwf, water is highly sensitive. (3) The collapsibility of humidification is strong at high pressure and weak at low pressure. The influence of humidification pressure is much more obvious at low initial moisture content than at high initial moisture content. (4) Under the same humidification water content, the humidification deformation decreases as the initial water content w0 increases. However, when w0<wf, the change in w0 has almost no effect on the development of humidification collapsibility deformation and can be normalized. (5) Under different pressures and initial moisture content, the δs' -(mP,w0?) of loess from Xi 'an and Lanzhou The linear relationship among m P,w) is very similar, that is, the law by which the development of humidification deformation is affected by structure is basically the same.

Compression tests were conducted on unshaped loess from Lanzhou and Xi 'an under different moisture contents. A quantitative structural parameter was defined based on the pore ratios of unshaped, reshaped and saturated loess under the same pressure, and the regularity of the structural parameter varying with pressure and moisture content was discussed. Research shows that :(1) By using the characteristics of structural parameters varying with moisture content and pressure to study the influence of structure on the compressibility and collapsibility of undisturbed loess, good regularity can be obtained. (2) Under the same moisture content, the structural parameters increase with the increase of pressure before the pressure reaches the structural strength. After reaching the structural strength, the increase in pressure causes the structural parameters to decrease significantly or increase slowly. (3) Under the same pressure, the structural parameters decrease very little before the moisture content reaches the initial moisture content ωf for structural failure. After reaching ωf, the moisture content increases, causing the structural parameters to decrease rapidly. ωf decreases with the increase of pressure. The change in moisture content at high pressure has a significant impact on structural parameters. (4) The relationship between the collapsibility coefficient and the structural parameter mp of loess in the two regions under different pressures or moisture contents can be normalized into a straight line, that is, the law of the collapsibility coefficient being affected by the structure is the same.

To address the challenges such as the depth of foundation treatment and the evaluation of collapsibility in areas with large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess, when the thickness of collapsible loess is greater than 36.5 The following immersion tests were conducted at the site of m: deep immersion load tests of foundation treated with compacted piles at different depths, deep immersion load tests of foundation treated with dynamic compaction in holes at different depths, and in-situ immersion tests without drilling water injection holes and embedding TDR moisture meters. The research results show that :(1) When treating large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess foundation with a depth of 6 to 12 meters and deep immersion, significant foundation subsidence occurs; When the height is 15 to 20 meters, the settlement of the foundation is relatively small. When the treatment depth is greater than 20 meters, foundation settlement can basically be ignored. (2) The moisture content of the soil above 22.5 to 25.0 meters in the immersion test pit increased rapidly, even reaching saturation. The moisture content of the soil below increased slowly, and basically no subsidence occurred. It is recommended that 22.5 to 25.0 meters be used as the critical depth for the foundation treatment and collapsibility evaluation of large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess. (3) For large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess foundations, after taking effective comprehensive treatment measures, the collapsibility of Class A buildings does not need to be completely eliminated, while the requirements for the remaining collapsibility of Class B and C buildings can be appropriately relaxed in accordance with the control suggestions. (4) Different regions and different microstructure types of soil should adopt different collapsibility coefficients s? To determine it, that is, 'the collapsibility coefficient s?'' =0.015' can continue to be used within a range of 15 meters from the base bottom surface to the base. For depths below 15 meters, the requirements can be appropriately relaxed. For zs?, adjust according to different depths. Making corrections can make the collapsibility evaluation of large-thickness self-weight collapsible loess more reasonable and effectively save a large amount of foundation treatment costs.

The triaxial shear test of unsaturated unstate Q3 loess under humidification was carried out by using the consolidated non-draining shear method. The relationship curves between the ultimate strength of collapsible loess and the initial moisture content and confining pressure were obtained, and the variation law of the shear strength of loess during the humidification process was analyzed. The results show that the strength envelope of unsaturated unshaped loess at the original water content is a broken line composed of two straight lines, and the strength envelope at high water content still conforms to the Mohr-Coulomb theory criterion. The cohesion of unsaturated loess decreases rapidly with the increase of the initial water content, while the internal friction Angle is less affected by the change of the initial water content. With the increase of the initial water content, the total stress index is always less than the corresponding effective stress index. It was also found that the relationship between the effective cohesion c 'of unsaturated loess and the initial water content w could be well fitted as an exponential function. Finally, the influence of the initial moisture content and confining pressure on the shear strength index was briefly analyzed.
